title: Kolrosing
subtitle: Incising fine lines and filling with pigment for surface decoration on spoons and treen
excerpt: Kolrosing is a Scandinavian surface-decoration technique in which fine lines are inscribed into the wood with a knife and then rubbed with ground coffee, charcoal, or pigment to fill the cuts and darken the design. The excess is wiped away, leaving the dark lines in the grooves. It suits spoons, bowls, and any smooth finished treen surface.
sourceNotes: Kolrosing is documented in Scandinavian folk-craft literature. The technique description draws on the general principles of incised-and-filled decoration as described in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for wood surface decoration.

content.text: Kolrosing requires a sharp knife and a steady hand, but no burning unit, no power tools, and no special equipment beyond what a spoon-carver already owns. The incised lines are very fine, 0.3 to 0.5 mm wide, made with the tip of a
detail knife
. The filling material is simply ground coffee, fine charcoal powder, or a natural earth pigment rubbed firmly into the cuts.
Kolrosing
is done after the spoon or piece of
treen
has been finished and sanded but before the final oil coat. The oil, applied after the pigment, seals the design in place and keeps it stable through washing.
When to use it

Kolrosing suits the handle and neck of a spoon, the outer surface of a small bowl, or any flat or gently curved treen surface that has been sanded smooth. It does not suit the bowl of a spoon (the concave surface makes the lines hard to control) or any very coarse-grained wood where the design lines will be obscured by the grain texture. Sycamore, birch, and beech are ideal; coarse-grained oak or ash makes fine lines less legible.
What you need

A detail knife or a sloyd knife with a sharp tip. The edge must be able to score a clean, fine line without tearing the wood fibres; strop before starting. Ground coffee (finely ground espresso is ideal; the fine particle size fills the line cleanly). Charcoal powder as an alternative for darker lines. Raw linseed oil to seal the finished design. A soft cloth for applying and wiping the pigment.
Step 1: Design and preparation

Sand the surface to at least 240 grit. Pencil the design lightly on the sanded surface. Simple geometric patterns (repeating chevrons, diamonds, border lines) and flowing organic motifs both work well. Keep the first project simple: a border line around the handle near the bowl transition, or a small repeating diamond pattern on the handle back. Complex designs require confident knife work; start with a pattern that uses straight or gently curving lines.
Step 2: Incise the lines

Hold the detail knife like a pencil, with the tip resting lightly on the wood. Draw the knife toward you along the pencilled line, cutting a single fine groove. The groove should be visible but very shallow, around 0.3 to 0.5 mm. Press just enough to cut through the surface fibres cleanly. Too much pressure makes a wide groove that holds too much pigment and gives a blurred result; too little pressure and the line barely registers.

For curved lines, rotate the piece rather than trying to curve the knife stroke. Short straight strokes that follow the curve give more control than one long curved stroke on most designs. Where lines intersect, make a stop cut at the intersection first, then incise toward it from each direction; this prevents the line from running past the corner.
Step 3: Fill with pigment

Once all the incised lines are complete, take a pinch of finely ground coffee or charcoal powder and rub it firmly into the incised surface with a fingertip or a folded piece of soft cloth. Work the pigment into every line by rubbing in circular motions. The lines will fill immediately; the surrounding surface will also darken.

Wipe the surplus pigment from the flat surfaces with a clean, dry cloth. Wipe firmly enough to clear the surface clean, but do not press so hard that you drag pigment out of the lines. The dark design lines should remain clearly visible against the pale wood surface after wiping.
Step 4: Seal with oil

Apply raw linseed oil to the entire piece immediately after the pigment has been wiped clean. Work the oil into the surface with a cloth, allow it to penetrate for an hour, then wipe off the surplus. The oil seals the pigment into the lines and prevents it from washing out in use. Allow to cure for two weeks before food contact.

items.fix: Strop the knife before starting. For cross-grain lines, make two lighter passes rather than one heavier one. Go with the grain direction on each individual section of the design even if it means interrupting the stroke.
items.cause: The knife tip was blunt, or the cut went against the grain in a cross-grain section of the design.
items.symptom: The design lines are ragged or torn rather than clean and sharp

items.fix: Sand the surface to 320 or 400 grit before the next kolrosing session. For an in-progress piece, a light 400-grit sand of the surface (not the incised lines) will remove the pigment from the flat areas.
items.cause: The surface was not sanded fine enough before kolrosing, leaving open grain pores that hold the pigment.
items.symptom: The pigment colours the surrounding wood surface and cannot be wiped away cleanly

items.fix: Re-incise the same lines with slightly more pressure to deepen them, then refill with pigment.
items.cause: The incised grooves were too shallow to hold enough pigment.
items.symptom: The design lines are very pale and not legible after filling
